overview: Waterside occupies southeastern Stamford where the city's industrial past meets its waterfront future. The neighborhood borders Long Island Sound to the east and abuts the Harbor Point redevelopment to the south — a transit-oriented, mixed-use district that has added approximately 2,750 residential units since its inception. Southfield Avenue is the commercial artery, lined with waterfront restaurants and new apartment buildings, while Kosciuszko Park and the recently renovated John J. Boccuzzi Park provide public shoreline access. Homes range from $400,000 multifamily properties to $800,000 renovated single-family residences, with the trajectory pointing upward as infrastructure investment continues.

children.text: Waterside's transformation is the most significant neighborhood-scale redevelopment story in Stamford. For much of the twentieth century, this stretch of shoreline was industrial — warehouses, manufacturing facilities, and working waterfront that kept residents separated from the harbor. The arrival of Harbor Point changed the equation. Built on a formerly contaminated industrial site south of Waterside's residential core, the development introduced thousands of residential units, retail storefronts, a boardwalk, and direct pedestrian connections to the water. That boardwalk now links to Kosciuszko Park, an eighteen-acre waterfront peninsula featuring a five-acre coastal meadow installed in 2010 that serves as a stopover for migrating birds in spring and fall.

John J. Boccuzzi Park, named after a Waterside resident who served on Stamford's Board of Representatives for more than forty years, is undergoing a sweeping renovation funded in part by a $1.8 million federal grant from the Outdoor Recreation Legacy Partnership. The improvements include a splash pad, a multi-use lawn, a redesigned playground, a tot lot, and new landscaping. SoundWaters, an environmental education organization, is constructing a 12,100-square-foot Harbor Center within the park — a project supported by a $3 million leadership gift from the Steven & Alexandra Cohen Foundation. When completed, the center will include beach restoration and expanded public waterfront access. These investments are not speculative; they are funded and under construction.

The residential fabric of Waterside is layered. Mid-century single-family homes — many with original clapboard or vinyl siding — sit alongside multifamily buildings, converted industrial structures, and newly constructed apartment complexes. Southfield Avenue carries the commercial energy, with restaurants and storefronts occupying ground-floor spaces beneath residential units. The neighborhood's density and mixed-use character distinguish it from Stamford's suburban tiers. Walking is the primary mode of local transit. Residents pass toy manufacturers, tech offices, and corner bodegas on the same block — an urban texture uncommon outside downtown Stamford.

The price range of $400,000 to $800,000 makes Waterside one of Stamford's most accessible coastal neighborhoods. Multifamily properties and condominiums occupy the lower end, while renovated single-family homes and newer construction approach the upper tier. Harbor Point's adjacent luxury rental market, where units command premium rents, establishes a price ceiling that pulls Waterside's ownership market upward over time. The Stamford Transportation Center is less than a mile north, Interstate 95 exits directly into the area, and Stamford Harbor's working and recreational waterfront spreads out along the eastern edge. For buyers willing to invest alongside ongoing public and private capital, Waterside offers a combination of price, location, and momentum that few Stamford neighborhoods can match.

highlights: Kosciuszko Park, an eighteen-acre waterfront peninsula, features a five-acre coastal meadow, crushed-stone walking paths, and panoramic views of Stamford Harbor
John J. Boccuzzi Park is receiving a $1.8 million federal renovation including a splash pad, playground, and the new SoundWaters Harbor Center
Harbor Point's mixed-use redevelopment has added approximately 2,750 residential units, a boardwalk, and retail storefronts directly adjacent to Waterside
Stamford Transportation Center is less than a mile north, providing Metro-North express service and Amtrak connections from the neighborhood's doorstep
housingCharacteristics:
Waterside's housing stock reflects the neighborhood's transitional identity. The oldest layer consists of mid-century single-family homes — Capes, small colonials, and modest two-story residences on compact lots, many still wearing original finishes. Multifamily properties, including duplexes and small apartment buildings, are interspersed throughout the residential streets and represent a significant share of available inventory.

Newer construction is visible along Southfield Avenue and the blocks closest to Harbor Point, where former industrial parcels have been converted into residential buildings with ground-floor retail. Condominium units in these developments offer modern finishes and, in some cases, harbor views. The price band of $400,000 to $800,000 reflects this variety — multifamily and original-condition homes anchor the lower range, while renovated single-family properties and newer condos approach the upper end. The neighborhood's housing diversity creates entry points across a wider buyer spectrum than most Stamford waterfront areas can offer.
marketNotes:
Waterside's market is driven by adjacency to Harbor Point and the ongoing public investment in waterfront infrastructure. Buyers at the lower end of the $400,000 to $600,000 range are typically first-time purchasers or investors acquiring multifamily properties with rental income potential. Above $600,000, owner-occupants seeking renovated single-family homes or newer condominiums dominate.

The neighborhood benefits from a pricing asymmetry: waterfront access and proximity to the Stamford Transportation Center at prices significantly below Shippan, Harbor Point rentals, and downtown Stamford condos. As Boccuzzi Park improvements and the SoundWaters Harbor Center reach completion, the value proposition strengthens further. Sellers who invest in cosmetic updates and accurate pricing tend to attract strong interest, particularly from buyers relocating from New York City who recognize the urban density and transit access as familiar territory.
locationAccess:
Waterside sits in southeastern Stamford with Interstate 95 running directly through the neighborhood's northern edge. Exit 9 provides immediate highway access, placing Greenwich fifteen minutes west and Norwalk fifteen minutes east. The Stamford Transportation Center — serving Metro-North Railroad, Amtrak, and Connecticut Transit buses — is less than a mile north, making Waterside one of the closest residential neighborhoods to Stamford's primary transit hub.

Harbor Point's boardwalk extends south along the waterfront, connecting to Kosciuszko Park and offering a two-mile flat walking route with harbor views. Southfield Avenue provides the neighborhood's commercial services, and downtown Stamford's restaurants, offices, and Stamford Town Center mall are within a five-to-ten-minute drive or a reasonable walk. The combination of highway, rail, and pedestrian infrastructure gives Waterside connectivity that rivals any neighborhood in the city.
seoTitle: Waterside, Stamford CT Real Estate & Homes for Sale
seoDescription: Explore Waterside homes in Stamford CT — harbor views, direct transit access, and new waterfront parks at accessible prices. Contact Matt Caiola today.

faqs.question: What is the price range for homes in Waterside, Stamford CT?
faqs.answer: Waterside homes and condos generally trade between $400,000 and $800,000. Multifamily properties and original-condition single-family homes occupy the lower end of that range, while renovated residences and newer condominium units with harbor views approach the upper tier. The neighborhood's proximity to Harbor Point's luxury rental market and ongoing public park investments support a strengthening price trajectory.

faqs.question: What waterfront parks are in the Waterside neighborhood?
faqs.answer: Waterside is served by two significant waterfront parks. Kosciuszko Park is an eighteen-acre peninsula with a five-acre coastal meadow, crushed-stone walking paths, picnic facilities, and panoramic Stamford Harbor views. John J. Boccuzzi Park, currently undergoing a federally funded renovation, will add a splash pad, playground, tot lot, multi-use lawn, and the new SoundWaters Harbor Center — a 12,100-square-foot environmental education facility with restored beach access.

faqs.question: How close is Waterside to the Stamford train station?
faqs.answer: The Stamford Transportation Center is less than a mile north of Waterside, making it one of the closest residential neighborhoods to Stamford's primary transit hub. The station serves Metro-North Railroad with express trains reaching Grand Central Terminal in under an hour, as well as Amtrak service along the Northeast Corridor. Connecticut Transit bus routes also operate from the station, connecting to regional destinations.
